D9 Biology L5 Evaluate Reference

Aequorea Jellyfish — Ca²⁺-Triggered Blue, GFP-Shifted Green

aequorin emits 470 nm blue when Ca²⁺ binds; in the jellyfish, the photon resonance-transfers to the green-fluorescent-protein partner GFP and the animal emits 509 nm green instead

Osamu Shimomura discovered aequorin (1962) and GFP from Aequorea victoria; Nobel 2008. Aequorin contains coelenterazine + Ca²⁺ binding sites. Free Ca²⁺ binds → conformational change → coelenterazine oxidises → 470 nm blue. In live jellyfish, aequorin and GFP are co-localised; resonance transfer shifts emission to 509 nm green.

Drag [Ca²⁺]. Below threshold (~10⁻⁷ M) nothing; above, brightness saturates. Toggle GFP-coupling on to shift emission from blue to green.
